1: Isaiah 18 opens the oracle about Cush (Ethiopia/Nubia): 'Woe to the land buzzing with wings beyond the rivers of Cush' (hoi erets tsiltsal kenafayim asher me'ever lenehare-Kush, woe O land of whirring/buzzing of wings that is beyond the rivers of Cush). The 'buzzing with wings' (tsiltsal kenafayim, the buzzing-insect sound of wings) may refer to the abundant insects of the Nile region (locusts, flies — cf. Isaiah 7:18-19 where YHWH whistles for Egypt's flies), or to the sound of swift messengers' feet, or to the sails/wings of the river craft. 'Beyond the rivers of Cush' (me'ever lenehare-Kush) — the land south of Egypt's Nile branches. During Isaiah's time (725-700 BCE), Egypt was ruled by the 25th Dynasty of Cushite/Nubian pharaohs: Piankhi/Piye (747-716), Shabaka (716-702), Shebitku (702-690), and Taharqa (690-664). 'Cush' thus refers to the rulers of both Nubia and Egypt.
2: 'It sends envoys by sea, in papyrus boats skimming the waters. Go, swift messengers, to a people tall and smooth-skinned, dreaded everywhere near and far — a mighty, dominating nation whose land is cut by rivers' — the Cushite envoys described: 'boshilach bayam tzirim uvekhele-gome, who sends envoys by sea, in papyrus vessels.' The papyrus boats (kele-gome, vessels of papyrus reed) were the characteristic Nile craft — light, fast, made from bundled papyrus. The description of the Cushites: 'tall and smooth-skinned' (memushshak umorah, smooth/sleek of skin), 'feared from near and far' (nora min-hu umehala'ah, feared from it and from afar), 'a mighty, dominating nation' (goi qav-qav umevusah, a line-measuring and trampling nation — meaning one that measures out territory and crushes resistance), 'whose land rivers divide' (asher baze'u nehroth artso, whose land rivers have cut/divided). The Nile and its branches created the geography of Nubia/Egypt.
3: 'All inhabitants of the world and all who dwell on earth — when a signal is hoisted on the mountains, look! When a trumpet is sounded, listen!' — the universal attention summoned: YHWH issues a call to the entire world (kulkhem yoshevei tevel veshokhnei erets, all you who dwell in the world and you who dwell on earth). The two signals: a nes (banner/signal) raised on the mountains — look! A shofar (ram's horn, the trumpet of assembly and alarm) — listen! The signal-and-trumpet combination is the standard military and prophetic assembly call (Isaiah 5:26: YHWH lifting a banner and whistling for the nations; Joel 2:1: 'Blow the trumpet in Zion, sound the alarm on my holy mountain'). All nations are being called to witness what YHWH is about to do regarding the Cushite-Egyptian power that the envoys represent.
4: 'For this is what the LORD said to me: I will stay quiet in my dwelling place and watch — like shimmering heat in the bright sun, like a cloud of dew in the heat of harvest' — the divine posture of quiet watching: 'ki koh amar YHWH elai, for thus the LORD said to me.' YHWH's description of his own posture: eshqotah ve'abbitah vimekoniy, 'I will be quiet and watch in my dwelling place.' The two similes for YHWH's barely-perceptible-yet-present posture: (1) 'like shimmering heat over clear light' (kecham tsach al-or, like heat in clear sunlight — the desert heat shimmer barely visible but definitely present); (2) 'like a cloud of dew in the heat of harvest' (ke'av tal bechor qatsir, like a cloud of dew in the heat of harvest — the early morning dew-mist barely visible as vapor before the sun burns it off). YHWH is not absent — he watches — but does not immediately intervene. This posture of divine patience before sudden intervention is the theological key to 701 BCE (Sennacherib's army at Jerusalem, then overnight destruction).
5: 'For before the harvest, once the blossom is done and the flower turns into a maturing grape, he will trim off the shoots with pruning knives and strip away and cut down the spreading branches' — the agricultural timing of divine intervention: 'ki lifnei qatsir kethom perach, for before the harvest when the blossom is finished.' The exact moment: when the blossom (perach) is over and the flower (nitsah) becomes a maturing/ripening grape (boser gomel, the sour-grape that is ripening). At the precise moment of near-maturity — the Cushite/Assyrian threat is fully developed and about to strike — YHWH acts: 'yikrath hazelazlim bemazemoroth veeth-hanesisim heser yatzil, he will cut the branches with pruning knives and the spreading branches he will strip away and cut down.' The vineyard imagery (YHWH as vinedresser, ch. Isaiah 5:1-7; Isaiah 27:2-6) applied to geopolitical pruning: the threatening power cut at the moment of apparent triumph.
6: 'They will all be abandoned to the mountain birds of prey and to the wild animals; the birds will summer on the carcasses and the beasts will winter on them' — the aftermath of divine pruning: the cut branches (the Cushite/Assyrian forces) left for the birds and beasts. 'They will be abandoned together' (ye'azvu yachdav) to the birds of prey of the mountains (le'ayit harim) and to the beasts of the land (velivhemeth ha'arets). The seasonal cycle of scavenging: 'the birds will summer on them' (veyatitz alehem ha'ayit, the summer-prey bird will summer on them) and 'all the beasts of the land will winter on them' (kol-behemat ha'arets alehem tacharopenah, all the beasts will winter on them). The historical referent is the Sennacherib defeat of 701 BCE — 185,000 soldiers left as carcasses overnight (2 Kings 19:35), then the 'summer' and 'winter' scavengers completing the process.
7: 'At that time a tribute offering will be brought to the LORD of hosts from the tall and smooth-skinned people — the nation feared everywhere, the mighty and dominating people whose land the great rivers divide — to Mount Zion, where the LORD of hosts has placed his name' — the oracle's positive conclusion: the judgment on Cushite power (expressed in Isaiah 18:5-6) leads to Cush voluntarily bringing tribute to YHWH at Zion. 'Ba'et hahi yuval-shay laYHWH tseva'ot, at that time a gift will be brought to the LORD of hosts' from the very people described in v2 (tall and smooth-skinned, feared, mighty, divided-by-rivers). The voluntary tribute from Egypt/Cush to Zion fulfills Psalms 68:31 ('princes come from Egypt; Cush will stretch out her hands to God') and Psalms 87:4 (Cush among those registered as knowing YHWH). The final destination: 'el har Tsiyon meqom shem YHWH tseva'ot, to Mount Zion, the place of the name of the LORD of hosts' — the ingathering of nations to YHWH's dwelling.
